text: Obstructed hemavagina and ipsilateral renal anomaly is a rare Mullerian duct anomaly with uterus didelphys, unilateral obstructed hemavagina, and ipsirenal agenesis. Patients with this anomaly usually present after menage with pelvic pain and a mass and rarely in later years with primary infertility strong suspicious or knowledge of this anomaly is essential for a precise diagnosis you we describe our technique for an emmy left colpo hysterectomy
The surgeon begins the procedure with an anterograde salpingectomy, using a 5mm ligation sealing device to efficiently perform the task.
The surgeon opens the round ligament by cutting its medial aspect and continues the dissection towards the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament.
The surgeon cuts the utero-ovarian ligament and the adnexal pedicle, guided by the assistant who demonstrates the correct dissecting plane.
The surgeon dissects the posterior and anterior leaves of the left broad ligament, carefully separating these structures to expose the underlying anatomical features.

The surgeon removes the fallopian tube to improve visibility, revealing a large left-sided hematocorpus with a diameter of ten centimeters. The hematocorpus is prominently visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the internal iliac artery and its bifurcation, then locates the uterine artery at its origin. The uterine artery is carefully cut to reduce perioperative bleeding.
The surgeon continues the dissection to circumscribe the large hematocorpus, revealing deep dissection planes and large vessels surrounding the obstructed vagina.
The surgeon identifies the superficial uterine vein and uses a ligature sealing device to control it, aiming to maintain a bloodless field for clear visualization of the dissection plane.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the left uterosacral ligament, circumscribing the large hematocorpus posteriorly. The vesicovaginal dissection proceeds as the bladder is pulled downwards to enhance exposure.
The surgeon controls the remnant part of the left uterosacral ligament, ensuring there is no risk to the ureter due to the patient's kidney agenesis.

The surgeon carefully dissects posteriorly to visualize the lower part of the hematocorpus, controlling the artery and vein surrounding it. The uterine artery is controlled at the isthmic uterine area to manage perioperative bleeding.
The surgeon cuts the left bladder pillar, located just above the large hematocorpus, using surgical instruments.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the paravesical fossa and controlling the superior vesical vein and artery using surgical instruments.
The surgeon continues the vesicovaginal dissection, progressively revealing the peak of the hematocompost, ensuring it is circumscribed by pulling the bladder downwards.
The surgeon removes the uterine corpus, providing a clearer view of the residual cervix and the obstructed left hemivagina. The supracervical hysterectomy procedure is visibly straightforward.

The surgeon uses Podzi forceps to manipulate the remnant cervix, opening it with a monopolar hook to access the obstructed left hemivagina. The fibrotic tissue of the hematocorpus is incised, revealing a large amount of hemociderin clot, which is carefully removed.
The surgeon rinses the obstructed left hemivagina, preparing for its removal. Two techniques are mentioned for this procedure: using a ligatrol sealing device or monopolar cutlery.

The surgeon uses a ligature for hemostasis and inserts a finger into the right vagina, which is visibly large and fibrotic. The finger is clearly seen inside the right functional vagina. The surgeon continues working on the left vagina with a monopolar hook, performing a marsupialization without closing the remnant vagina.

The surgeon places the clot inside an endobag and removes it through the left iliac operative trocar. The anterior aspect of the obstructed vagina is removed, followed by the left supracervical hysterectomy. The remnant vagina is visible, with a finger inserted in the right vagina.

The surgeon uses a fifteen millimeter emulsilator to finalize the surgery, revealing the right ureter, right hemiuterus, and right hemivagina. The left hemivagina is left open inside the pelvis. An ovariopexy is performed to decrease the risk of ovarian torsion.
